---
title: "Whip Nutrush (Scleria triglomerata)"
slug: "scleria-triglomerata"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/475059389/medium.jpeg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Whip Nutrush"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Liliopsida"
tax_order: "Poales"
tax_family: "Cyperaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Liliopsida", "Poales", "Cyperaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/475059389/medium.jpeg" alt="Whip Nutrush" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/coreytcallaghan">
coreytcallaghan</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/264558389">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Liliopsida
-   **Order:** Poales
-   **Family:** Cyperaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=141078)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Uncommon species in Florida found in moist to wet habitats.

## Natural history notes

A grass-like wetland plant characterized by its slender, often arching stems and small clusters of nut-like fruits. In Florida, it is found in moist to wet habitats such as flatwoods, bogs, and seepage areas, often in sandy soils. It grows during the warmer months and contributes to plant diversity and structure in wetland communities.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/141078-Scleria-triglomerata)
-   [Local profile](https://florida.plantatlas.usf.edu/plant/species/2055)
